objective: Establish Nate's 2025 life: the dead-end job, the divorce, the apartment, the obsessive folder on Dominic Altieri
opposition: Nate's own inertia and the banality of his resentment — he has nursed this hatred so long it has become furniture
turn: Nate opens the ALTIERI folder and we see the depth of his obsession — photos, records, a timeline of grievances — revealing this is not casual bitterness but an organized pathology
consequence_cost: The reader understands that Nate has sacrificed years of his life to this fixation; the cost is already paid

objective: Show the vibe-coding process: the AI sessions, the Amazon components, the incremental discovery that the device manipulates spacetime, the decision to use it for murder
opposition: Nate's ignorance — he doesn't understand the physics, the AI's warnings are in jargon he skims past, the device is held together with code he can't read
turn: The device works in ways Nate didn't expect during testing — a coffee mug ages overnight, timestamps scramble — and Nate realizes he has built something real. The turn is from confused tinkering to deliberate weaponization
consequence_cost: Nate has committed to murder as a plan; the vibe-coded device is unstable and poorly understood; he has ignored every warning

objective: The activation: Nate targets Dominic in 2005, activates the machine, and wakes up in Dominic's body
opposition: The machine itself — it doesn't do what Nate expected. Instead of physical transport, consciousness transfer
turn: Blackout, then sensation: wrong body, wrong room, wrong decade. Nate opens eyes that aren't his. Clock reads 3:47 AM. A woman breathes beside him. July 11, 2005
consequence_cost: Nate's 2025 body is gone, the machine is destroyed, the transfer is permanent. He has killed Dominic by overwriting his consciousness — murder by deletion. There is no return

objective: Nate gets out of bed, explores the bathroom and apartment, inventories Dominic's life through physical objects
opposition: The physical alienation of being in the wrong body — wrong hands, wrong face, wrong muscle memory. No smartphone, no internet worth using, a flip phone he can barely operate
turn: The mirror scene: Nate sees Dominic's face and must accept that this is now his face. The inventory reveals the contours of a life — wallet, keys, a percolator he doesn't know how to use
consequence_cost: Nate's technological helplessness in 2005 is established as an ongoing constraint; his identity as Nate Lazar is functionally erased

objective: Sarah's morning routine; Nate's first interaction with someone who thinks he's Dominic; Sarah leaves and Nate sits with the weight of what he's done
opposition: Sarah's unselfconscious intimacy — she kisses his cheek, calls him Dom, moves through the apartment with the ease of someone who knows this space and this man
turn: Sarah notices nothing catastrophic, which is itself the turn: the impersonation is viable, which means it must continue. After she leaves, the relief curdles into horror — this can work, and that means he's trapped in it
consequence_cost: Nate is committed to the impersonation. Sarah's trust is a resource he's exploiting and a debt that will compound

objective: Nate drives through Bridgeport for the first time, navigating 2005 without GPS, registering the temporal displacement through physical details
opposition: Spatial disorientation — no GPS, a paper atlas, different storefronts, the sheer sensory strangeness of a world without smartphones
turn: The turn is accumulative rather than punctual: the Blockbuster, the gas prices, the newspaper confirming the date — each detail hammers in the permanence of Nate's situation
consequence_cost: The emotional cost of temporal displacement: Nate is twenty years from everything he knows, in a body that isn't his, with a paper map

objective: Nate drives to Eastbridge Plaza and sees his father alive through the window of Lazar's Hardware
opposition: Nate's own paralysis — he cannot enter the store, cannot approach Mort, cannot be recognized in this body
turn: Seeing Mort: alive, healthy, talking to a customer, hands moving the way Nate remembers. The turn is purely emotional — the abstraction of revenge gives way to the embodied reality of a living father. Nate sits in the car for forty minutes, unable to move
consequence_cost: Nate's revenge mission has delivered him to the one outcome he didn't plan for: proximity to what he lost, without the ability to claim it. The cost is exquisite, ongoing, and has no relief

objective: Nate arrives at Kassos Development for the first time, meets Ray, and begins faking Dominic's professional life
opposition: The office demands specific knowledge Nate doesn't have — software, clients, project history, professional relationships
turn: Ray greets him warmly and starts catching him up on work Nate doesn't understand. Nate asks questions that surprise Ray — basic things Dominic would know
consequence_cost: Nate's incompetence creates immediate professional risk; Ray's confusion is an early crack in the impersonation

objective: Meeting with Victor; Nate's first exposure to the charismatic, paternal authority that controlled Dominic's professional life
opposition: Victor's sharp intelligence beneath the warmth — he assesses people constantly, and the man in front of him is not performing at the expected level
turn: Victor greets Nate warmly but watches him through the meeting with a quality of attention that isn't casual. Nate stumbles through project updates he doesn't understand
consequence_cost: The professional impersonation is fragile; Victor is established as a perceptive, potentially dangerous figure

objective: Nate at Dominic's desk, opening the Eastbridge Plaza file, discovering his father's name on the tenant list
opposition: The file itself — the physical evidence that Dominic's desk processed the destruction of Nate's family
turn: Nate opens the folder and finds: site plans, demolition timeline, tenant list. Morton Lazar, Lazar's Hardware, Unit 7. He is sitting at the desk where it happened
consequence_cost: The discovery transforms the office from a place of comic imposture to a site of moral reckoning. Nate is now the custodian of the project that will destroy his father's store

objective: An evening at home with Sarah: cooking, conversation, the texture of cohabitation. Sarah references shared history Nate doesn't have
opposition: Sarah's casual references to a shared past — Providence, a fight about Renata, concert tickets — each one a landmine in the impersonation
turn: Nate absorbs and stores references, gets some wrong. Sarah gives him a look — not suspicious yet, but cataloging something. The turn: Sarah observes that Dominic has been softer lately, more present. She means it as a compliment. The imposter is accidentally a better partner
consequence_cost: The cruelest irony is established: Sarah may prefer the man who replaced her boyfriend. Nate's deception is producing a relationship that is, in some ways, better than the real one — which makes it worse

objective: First sexual encounter between Nate and Sarah — the specific strangeness and intimacy of sex in a borrowed body
opposition: The dissociation of inhabiting a body that knows this woman in ways Nate doesn't — muscle memory, reflexive responses, learned patterns of touch. Nate is present and absent simultaneously
turn: Sarah falls asleep with her hand on his chest. Nate lies awake. The turn is from the physical act to the moral aftermath: this was not rape in any legible way, but it was fraud of the most intimate kind
consequence_cost: Nate's relationship with Sarah is now sexual, which means the deception involves her body as well as her trust. There is no retreating to a safe distance

objective: Driving to the Altieri house; Renata's greeting; navigating the family geography through photos, smells, and the architecture of a home Nate has never entered
opposition: Renata's overwhelming personality and the density of family history embedded in every surface, every reference, every unfinished argument
turn: The house tour: Renata at the door, the smell of garlic and pork, photos everywhere. Nate studies family geography covertly. The turn is tonal — the Altieri home is nothing like the corporate villain's lair Nate imagined
consequence_cost: Nate's internal narrative about Dominic is eroded by domestic reality. The family is messy, warm, working-class — the opposite of the monster factory Nate constructed

objective: Sunday dinner: Tommy's arrival, the money request, the family chaos, Renata's parting observation
opposition: Tommy's charm and desperation; Renata's controlling love; the family's practiced navigation of each other's patterns
turn: Tommy asks for fifteen hundred, gets six hundred. The dinner is loud and overlapping and genuine. At the door, Renata says: 'You seem different. Good different.' — Nate-as-Dominic is inadvertently a kinder version, again
consequence_cost: Tommy's gambling debts are established as an ongoing drain. Nate has spent money from Dominic's account. Renata's emotional claim on Dominic/Nate creates a recurring obligation

objective: Nate alone in the office after hours, reading the complete Eastbridge Plaza file: financials, site plans, permits, tenant notification letters
opposition: The file itself — each document adding weight to the countdown. The notification letter signed by Dominic, addressed to Morton Lazar
turn: Nate finds the demolition date: December 28, 2005. Five months away. He holds the notification letter — his own handwriting now, Dominic's signature — and understands the timeline in his body
consequence_cost: The deadline transforms the novel's pressure from existential to operational. Nate has five months. The letter in his hand is both evidence of what Dominic did and a mirror for what Nate will be asked to do

objective: Nate at Gail's diner: the turkey club, the gossip, establishing himself as an anonymous regular in the strip mall community
opposition: None in a dramatic sense — this is a scene of connection, not opposition. The only pressure is internal: Nate knows this community is doomed
turn: Gail talks. Nate listens. He learns the strip mall ecosystem: Al the barber, Mrs. Park, Cesar. These are real people with specific lives, not line items on a tenant list
consequence_cost: Nate's attachment to the community deepens; these are no longer abstractions but people he'll watch lose their livelihoods

objective: Walking the strip mall: the barber, the dry cleaner, the bodega. Nate as anonymous observer of a place that exists in his memory as already destroyed
opposition: Internal: the dissonance between memory (demolished) and present (alive, functioning, full of people)
turn: Nate sees the strip mall as it was, not as he remembers it — full, messy, alive. The turn is perceptual: this place is not a symbol of his father's destruction but a living community
consequence_cost: The emotional stakes of the demolition escalate — this is not just Mort's store but a web of interconnected lives

objective: First real conversation with Mort: the handshake, small talk, Mort mentioning the redevelopment and naming 'some developer named Altieri'
opposition: Nate's need to remain anonymous versus his desperate desire to connect with his father; the specific agony of hearing Mort name Dominic as the destroyer
turn: The handshake: Nate holding his father's hand for the first time in nineteen years, wearing the face of the man Mort blames. Mort says Altieri's name. Nate absorbs it
consequence_cost: Nate is now known to Mort as Dom — a stranger who works nearby. The connection will deepen. Mort's naming of Altieri as the enemy, to Altieri's face, is a wound that will compound

objective: Nate pitches Victor on delaying or modifying the Eastbridge demolition, armed with research but lacking real expertise
opposition: Victor's intelligence and institutional knowledge — he knows the project better than Nate ever will, and he recognizes amateurish arguments immediately
turn: Victor dismantles Nate's pitch point by point with gentle, devastating precision. Then shifts register: 'Are you feeling all right, Dom?' — concern as threat
consequence_cost: Nate's internal sabotage strategy has failed publicly. Victor is now watching him. The professional margin for error has narrowed sharply

objective: Aftermath: Ray confronting Nate about the failed pitch; Nate at home with Sarah's quiet one-word exchange
opposition: Ray's direct, worried confrontation: 'What the hell was that?' — loyalty strained by confusion. Sarah's 'Fine' answer, the silence that follows
turn: Ray's question hangs. Nate deflects. The scene with Sarah is deliberately compressed — she asks, he answers, silence. The turn is in what isn't said
consequence_cost: Ray's trust is eroded but not broken — a finite resource being spent. Sarah's silence is louder than questions

objective: Marco's visit: Nate bluffs through an evening of shared memories he doesn't have with Dominic's college friend
opposition: Marco's expectation of intimacy and shared history — he references specific events, inside jokes, mutual acquaintances
turn: Nate nods and laughs at references he can't place. He survives. Marco says 'You seem different, man. Calmer' — another person attributing the impersonation to improvement
consequence_cost: The near-miss establishes a pattern: Nate can survive individual encounters but each one depletes a finite reserve of luck

objective: Sarah asks about Cape Cod — the first time she catches a factual gap rather than a behavioral anomaly
opposition: Sarah's memory: specific, detailed, testing. She mentions a restaurant and a scene Dominic made about the oysters
turn: Nate doesn't know the restaurant, the oysters, or the scene. His cover story is thin. Sarah's eyes narrow — this is a factual miss, not a mood shift
consequence_cost: The register of Sarah's suspicion shifts from emotional (he seems different) to evidentiary (he doesn't remember things)

objective: A client call about a past deal Nate knows nothing about; Ray in the doorway noting the gap
opposition: The client's assumption that Dominic remembers the terms of a deal he closed six months ago. Ray overhearing the stumble
turn: Nate stammers through the call. Ray appears: 'You didn't know who that was.' Not a question. A statement
consequence_cost: Professional exposure is imminent. Ray's observation is the closest anyone has come to confronting the impersonation directly

objective: Nate discovers Dominic's sent emails showing that Dominic fought the Eastbridge demolition from inside and lost
opposition: The emails themselves — each one a piece of evidence that Dominic was not the villain Nate constructed. The opposition is internal: Nate's foundational story versus the documentary record
turn: Dominic proposed alternatives, cited tenants by name (including Morton Lazar), and argued for a less destructive approach. Victor shut him down. Dominic's last email: 'I understand. I'll handle the notifications.' Nate realizes he killed a man who was trying to help
consequence_cost: Nate's moral framework collapses. The hatred that organized his adult life, that drove the time machine, that justified murder — was aimed at the wrong man. There is no way to undo this and no story that makes it bearable

objective: An ordinary evening that deteriorates: Sarah's accumulated frustrations surface when Nate opens the wrong drawer
opposition: Three months of anomalies — wrong drawers, flinching at his name, stopped habits, missed memories — compressed into a single confrontation. Sarah is armed with evidence Nate cannot refute
turn: Sarah begins cataloging: the drawers, the flinching, the stopped notes, the Tuesday calls to Renata that no longer happen. Each item is precise. The turn: this is not a fight about a drawer but an indictment of everything Nate has failed to perform
consequence_cost: Sarah's trust in the relationship is damaged. Nate's deflections ('I'm going through something') are true but functionally identical to lies

objective: The aftermath of the fight: Sarah on the couch, Nate in the bed, Nate considering and rejecting full disclosure
opposition: Nate's desire to confess versus the certainty that the truth — time travel, consciousness transfer, murder — would sound insane and end everything
turn: Sarah sleeps on the couch. Nate lies in the bed that isn't his and considers telling her everything. The turn: he decides he can't, not because the truth is unbelievable (though it is) but because telling her would make what he's done real in a way he's not ready to face
consequence_cost: The decision to continue the deception is itself a new betrayal, made with full knowledge of its cost. Nate has chosen the lie over the relationship — which is a choice that will deteriorate both

objective: Nate enters Lazar's Hardware for the first time: the sensory experience of a space he remembers from childhood, now encountered as a stranger
opposition: The collision of memory and present experience — the store smells the same, the inventory is similar, but Nate must experience it as a first-time customer. The constraint: he cannot show recognition
turn: Nate buys a level. Mort talks about the store with pride — the specialty fasteners, the sixteen years. The turn is in Nate's hands: holding the level, remembering learning to use one at eight years old, Mort's hands over his
consequence_cost: Nate is inside the store, connecting with his father over objects that carry childhood memory. Each moment of connection is also a moment of concealment

objective: Mort tells the stranger about the redevelopment; Nate makes an impulsive promise to help
opposition: Mort's resigned description of the demolition — not angry, tired — and Nate's inability to reveal who he is or what he knows. The double bind: he works for the company destroying the store
turn: Nate says: 'I work in real estate. I could look into it.' The words arrive before the thought. Mort looks at this stranger and says: 'I'd appreciate that.' Nate walks out carrying a level and a promise
consequence_cost: The promise creates a new obligation that conflicts with Nate's position at Kassos Development. He has committed to helping his father while employed by the man destroying his father's livelihood

objective: Nate presents a revised, better-prepared proposal to Victor for modifying the Eastbridge demolition
opposition: Victor's institutional knowledge and the depth of the commitments behind the project — investors, political connections, contracts
turn: Victor listens more carefully than before but then explains the full machinery: committed capital, the city council rezoning, Councilman Dreher's brother-in-law, handshake agreements. The turn: Victor reveals the system is bigger than any individual decision
consequence_cost: Nate understands the trap Dominic was in — not a villain making choices but a man inside a machine. The demolition cannot be stopped from inside Kassos Development

objective: Victor's warning: the implicit threat to Dominic's career, and the framing of complicity as pragmatism
opposition: Victor's shift from educator to enforcer — the warmth receding, the steel underneath. 'I'm a cog, Dom. A well-paid cog. So are you.'
turn: Victor's self-description reframes the moral landscape. He's not defending evil — he's describing a system. And the system has rules: cooperate or be replaced. The demolition proceeds either way
consequence_cost: Nate's professional position is explicitly threatened. His last avenue for internal change is closed. Victor's honesty is more devastating than hostility would be

objective: Tommy arrives at 11 PM with a split lip and a four-thousand-dollar gambling debt; Nate must decide how much of Dominic's resources to spend on a dead man's brother
opposition: Tommy's desperation: genuine fear underneath the charm, the habitual optimism about the next game. The financial constraint: Dominic's checking has twenty-two hundred, savings has eight hundred
turn: Tommy asks for four thousand. Nate has three thousand total. The turn: Nate gives him everything — empties both accounts. The decision is not generous but trapped: this is what Dominic would have done, and Nate is Dominic
consequence_cost: Nate is now broke. The financial cushion that made survival possible is gone. The brotherhood with Tommy is both inherited and genuine, which makes it costly in a way that pure obligation wouldn't be

objective: Aftermath: Tommy's departure, Sarah's quiet solidarity
opposition: Tommy's hug — 'thanks, Dom' — genuine, heavy. Sarah in the doorway, having heard everything
turn: Tommy leaves. Sarah pours a drink. She sits with Nate. No I told you so. The scene is quiet — two people absorbing a familiar crisis with practiced exhaustion
consequence_cost: Sarah's response reveals the depth of this recurring pattern. Tommy's debts are an old wound in this household. The moment of solidarity between Nate and Sarah is real and earned

objective: The final demolition meeting: timeline locked, paperwork queued. Gail reporting that Mort and Linda are packing the store
opposition: The institutional machinery in its terminal phase — no more proposals, no more arguments, just logistics. Gail's report adds embodied reality to the bureaucratic countdown
turn: The meeting is procedural: December 28, contractor confirmed, permits finalized. At the diner, Gail says Mort and Linda are boxing up inventory, their voices flat. The turn is the collision of institutional process and human cost
consequence_cost: The demolition is now operationally irreversible. Nate's last informational hope — that some procedural error might delay it — is gone

objective: Nate alone with the three options: refuse, sign, or warn. Interior reckoning. He signs
opposition: Nate's own moral framework — the collision of who he wanted to be with who he actually is when the choice arrives. Every option is a form of loss
turn: Nate thinks about Dominic making this choice. He understands why. He signs the paperwork — not with anguish but with the flat compliance of a man doing what the job requires. The turn: he has become Dominic in the way that matters most
consequence_cost: Nate has chosen complicity. The signature is both practical (keeps job, keeps life) and moral (participates in destruction). The cost is his last claim to being different from the man he killed

objective: The morning of the demolition: the street, the crowd, the former tenants gathering to watch
opposition: The physical reality of demolition — machines, dust, cold air. The crowd's grief and anger, which Nate must witness while carrying a signature that authorized it
turn: The crowd gathers: Gail, Al, Mrs. Park, Cesar. They stand in the cold. The turn is communal — these are the people Nate has come to know, and they are losing their livelihoods while he watches in the body of the man who signed the papers
consequence_cost: The community Nate joined as an anonymous regular is destroyed. The social bonds forged over lunch counters and strip mall sidewalks are severed

objective: Mort and Linda arrive. The demolition proceeds. Nate watches from across the street
opposition: Mort's composure — he watches without crying, jaw set, the specific discipline of a man refusing to break in public. Linda cries. Nate's concealment: he is Dominic's face, twenty yards away
turn: The wrecking crew works. The walls come down. Gail leaves when her sign falls. Mort watches the whole thing. The turn is in what doesn't happen: no confrontation, no catharsis, no heroic intervention. Just machines eating a building
consequence_cost: Nate witnesses his family's destruction from the perpetrator's position. The machine screw in the rubble — a left-hand thread, Mort's specialty stock — becomes a physical token of what's lost

objective: Aftermath: the empty lot, the silence, the drive home, Sarah's question
opposition: The void where the strip mall was. The drive home. Sarah's ordinary question about his day
turn: Nate drives home through streets that feel different now. Sarah is making dinner. She asks how his day was. He says 'Fine.' The turn is in the gap between the word and its meaning — the widest gap in the novel
consequence_cost: The emotional cost is deferred. Nate has absorbed the demolition without processing it. The compression into 'Fine' is a delayed detonation

objective: Weeks passing: the new development, Nate and Sarah's settling, the community adapting. Nate visiting Mort's garage regularly, noticing the signs of decline
opposition: Time and Mort's deterioration — quieter, slower, Linda's watchful fear. Nate can see what's coming because he's lived through it once before
turn: The new development rises on the empty lot. Nate and Sarah find a rhythm that isn't Dominic's relationship but isn't nothing. The turn: Nate notices Mort's hands shaking — not from cold, from something inside — and recognizes the early signs of the depression that will, in the original timeline, kill him
consequence_cost: Nate is watching his father approach the edge, knowing what lies beyond it, unable to name what he knows or explain how he knows it

objective: Final scene: Nate driving to the garage, looking at Dominic's hands, walking in. Mort's greeting. The finishing nails. The ending
opposition: Nate's knowledge of what's coming versus his inability to fix it through anything other than presence — showing up, buying things, maintaining connection
turn: Nate parks, looks at Dominic's hands — scar, softened calluses, the hands that signed the order and hold Sarah at night. He walks in. The bell chimes. Mort looks up: 'Hey, Dom. What do you need today?' Nate picks up finishing nails. 'Just these.' The novel ends mid-act: not saving, not confessing, just present
consequence_cost: The ending's cost is its openness: the reader knows what happened to Mort in the original timeline, and the novel refuses to confirm whether Nate's presence changes anything. The finishing nails are small and insufficient and ongoing — which is the only kind of help available
